title: CFAP47 (Cilia-and-Flagella-Associated Protein 47)
content:
Function: CFAP47 encodes a protein localized to the sperm flagellum, essential for tail assembly. It likely acts as a structural component or assembly factor of the flagellar axoneme. Recent studies show CFAP47 interacts with WDR87 in the sperm tail midpiece to facilitate correct flagellar formation . Mutations in CFAP47 cause multiple morphological abnormalities of the sperm flagella (MMAF) and immotile sperm, leading to male infertility .
Expression: CFAP47 is predominantly expressed in the testis (particularly in post-meiotic spermatids) and in other ciliated cells (e.g. respiratory epithelium) . It localizes to the sperm flagellum, especially the midpiece region .
Plausible Drive Mechanism: As an X-linked gene, CFAP47 could confer a post-meiotic advantage to X-bearing sperm. An X-bearing spermatid can produce CFAP47 protein to stabilize its developing flagellum, whereas a Y-bearing spermatid (lacking CFAP47) must rely on protein shared via intercellular bridges. Any insufficiency in Y-bearing spermatids may result in structurally weaker or less motile sperm. Thus, X-bearing sperm have more robust tails and better motility , skewing fertilization success towards X sperm. This segregation distortion would manifest as a female-biased sex ratio and enhanced transmission of the CFAP47 allele. Indeed, human and mouse evidence shows CFAP47 is critical for sperm motility and fertility , consistent with the idea that its presence (or optimal dosage) improves sperm performance.
Supporting Literature: Liao et al. (2022) identified CFAP47 as a flagellar protein required for assembling the sperm midpiece; loss of CFAP47 destabilized WDR87 and impaired tail formation . Liu et al. (2021) demonstrated that hemizygous CFAP47 mutations cause immotile, malformed sperm and X-linked infertility in men and mice . This highlights how an active CFAP47 allele can be beneficial to sperm function.

title: DDX3X (ATP-Dependent RNA Helicase DDX3X)
content:
Function: DDX3X is an RNA helicase involved in mRNA metabolism and translation initiation. It unwinds RNA secondary structures and participates in processes ranging from translation regulation to stress-granule assembly. In general, DDX3X (the X-linked paralog of the Y-linked DDX3Y) is a ubiquitously expressed enzyme essential for cellular growth. It can regulate translation of specific mRNAs and has roles in cell cycle and innate antiviral responses. Notably, DDX3X binds RNA and ATP and exhibits helicase activity to remodel ribonucleoprotein complexes .
Expression: DDX3X is expressed in many tissues, including the testis . In the male germline, DDX3Y is expressed in pre-meiotic spermatogonia, whereas DDX3X expression becomes more prominent in later stages (it escapes meiotic sex-chromosome inactivation to some extent) . Mouse studies show Ddx3x is present in germ cells (spermatogonia and possibly spermatids) and is required for early development (as Ddx3x-null males are embryonic lethal) . In humans, DDX3X is broadly expressed and likely contributes to spermatogenic cell function alongside its Y homolog.
Plausible Drive Mechanism: The interplay between DDX3X and DDX3Y is a classic example of X–Y genomic conflict. In males, both paralogs are present, but their temporal expression differs. During meiosis, DDX3Y is silenced (with the rest of the Y chromosome), while DDX3X may continue or resume expression post-meiotically . An X-bearing spermatid thus has a functional DDX3 helicase (DDX3X) in the haploid phase, potentially ensuring efficient mRNA translation for spermiogenesis. A Y-bearing spermatid, in contrast, cannot produce DDX3Y post-meiotically (and DDX3X is absent), so it must rely on residual protein or compensatory mechanisms. This could create a subtle functional disparity: X-bearing cells might more effectively translate key proteins during spermatid elongation and maturation. For example, transcripts required for tail formation or chromatin condensation might be more efficiently unwound and translated in the presence of DDX3X. Moreover, recent work suggests DDX3X can modulate DDX3Y levels (in somatic cells, DDX3X suppresses DDX3Y expression) , raising the possibility that DDX3X in an X-bearing germ cell could inhibit any persisting DDX3Y in a neighboring Y-bearing cell connected by cytoplasmic bridges. The net effect would be to disadvantage Y-spermatids. In summary, DDX3X’s continued function in late spermatogenesis may enhance X-sperm viability or quality relative to Y-sperm, contributing to a mild segregation distortion favoring the X chromosome.
Supporting Literature: Studies in humans and mice illustrate DDX3X/DDX3Y’s redundancy and unique roles. DDX3Y (Y-linked) is crucial for human spermatogenesis (AZFa deletions removing DDX3Y cause infertility), but in mice its loss is compensated by Ddx3x . Notably, human DDX3X and DDX3Y are expressed in different germ cell stages: DDX3Y predominantly in pre-meiotic cells, and DDX3X in post-meiotic cells . This compartmentalized expression supports the idea that DDX3X gives X-bearing spermatids a translational edge when forming functional sperm. While DDX3X knock-out is embryonic lethal in mice, chimeric studies showed that spermatogenesis can proceed with a Ddx3x/Ddx3y double knockout in germ cells (mice remained fertile) . This implies robust compensation (e.g., by the murine-specific D1Pas1 gene ). Nonetheless, the nuanced expression differences between DDX3X and DDX3Y in humans support a model in which DDX3X activity in haploid cells could subtly enhance X-sperm competitiveness.

title: DIAPH2 (Diaphanous-Related Formin 2)
content:
Function: DIAPH2 is a formin protein involved in actin cytoskeleton remodeling. It is the human homolog of Drosophila diaphanous, a gene required for cytokinesis. Formins like DIAPH2 nucleate and elongate actin filaments and also coordinate microtubules. DIAPH2 participates in diverse processes: it helps build actin networks, assists in cell polarity, and is implicated in vesicular trafficking and cell division. In humans, DIAPH2 is notable for its role in ovarian function (loss-of-function mutations cause premature ovarian failure) . In mice, related formins (mDia1/3) in Sertoli cells are required for proper spermatogenesis, as they maintain the actin structure of the Sertoli-germ cell junctional complex .
Expression: DIAPH2 is expressed broadly (low levels in many tissues) , but it has known importance in reproductive tissues. It is expressed in oocytes and is essential for female meiosis (the gene name comes from Drosophila diaphanous mutants that were sterile) . In the testis, DIAPH2 and other formins are present in spermatogenic and somatic cells; formins help organize the actin cytoskeleton during spermiogenesis and at Sertoli cell junctions . There is no strong testis-specific enrichment of DIAPH2 mRNA, likely due to redundancy with other formins, but its actin-remodeling function is certainly utilized during germ cell division and sperm head/tail formation.
Plausible Drive Mechanism: As an X-linked formin, DIAPH2 could influence meiotic drive by affecting cell division asymmetry or gamete morphology. During male meiosis and spermiogenesis, precise actin dynamics are needed for cytokinesis and nuclear shaping. In Drosophila, diaphanous mutants fail at the final cytokinesis of spermatogenesis, producing multinucleated spermatids and sterility . If a similar mechanism exists in mammals, a functional DIAPH2 allele might ensure successful spermatocyte divisions and separation of haploid cells. In a scenario where one allele (e.g., on the X) is active and a competing allele is absent or less effective (no equivalent on Y), the presence of DIAPH2 could prevent cytokinetic defects or malformations preferentially in X-bearing cells. For example, an X-bearing secondary spermatocyte might complete meiosis II more reliably (producing a viable spermatid) than a Y-bearing secondary spermatocyte with suboptimal formin activity. Additionally, DIAPH2 may assist in shaping the sperm head and connecting the acrosome to the nucleus; any subtle defects in Y-bearing spermatids lacking DIAPH2 might lead to slightly lower quality sperm (e.g., misshapen heads that are outcompeted at fertilization). Thus, while DIAPH2 is not overtly required (mice lacking Scml1 dense body, a structure containing DIAPH2’s partners, are fertile ), its optimal function could tilt the balance in favor of X-sperm, especially under competitive conditions.
Supporting Literature: The role of diaphanous homologs in male gametogenesis is well-established in insects and hinted in mammals. In Drosophila, mutated dia causes failure of cytokinesis in males, leading to large spermatids and male sterility . Human DIAPH2 was first identified via a translocation in a woman with ovarian failure, drawing parallels between diaphanous function in gametogenesis across sexes . Moreover, Sakamoto et al. (2018) showed that loss of formin mDia1/3 in mouse Sertoli cells disrupted germ cell attachment and spermatogenesis , underscoring the importance of actin regulation in male fertility. These findings support the notion that DIAPH2, through actin and possibly microtubule regulation, could affect the success of gamete formation in a way that might favor cells (or sperm) that carry the gene.

title: DYNLT3 (Dynein Light Chain, Tctex-Type 3)
content:
Function: DYNLT3 is a light-chain component of the cytoplasmic dynein motor complex. It belongs to the Tctex-type dynein light chains and has roles in intracellular transport and cell division. Specifically, DYNLT3 can bind dynein intermediate chains and also interact with the spindle checkpoint protein Bub3 . Through these interactions, it helps attach dynein motors to kinetochores and is involved in chromosome alignment and segregation. In cell division, a subpopulation of dynein containing DYNLT3 is important for proper congression of chromosomes and for transporting checkpoint signaling proteins away from the kinetochore . In summary, DYNLT3 links the motor machinery to mitotic/meiotic spindle apparatus, contributing to faithful chromosome segregation .
Expression: DYNLT3 is expressed in many tissues (“ubiquitous” expression), consistent with its general role in cell division. However, it was originally named Tctex1-like due to homology with a testis-expressed gene in mice, and its gene (on Xp21) is in a region enriched for male reproduction functions. It is present in male germ cells as well as somatic cells. Notably, in mouse oocytes, Dnylt3 is required for meiotic spindle alignment , implying it is active during meiosis I. In spermatocytes, by analogy, it likely participates in organizing the spindle and ensuring homologous chromosomes segregate correctly. DYNLT3 mRNA is detected broadly, and protein is found in dividing spermatogonial cells and possibly spermatocytes (though specific staining data in testis are limited).
Plausible Drive Mechanism: Being X-linked, DYNLT3 could cause meiotic drive through non-disjunction or biased segregation if its function is asymmetric between X-bearing and Y-bearing spermatocytes. During meiosis I, the X and Y chromosomes form the sex chromosome bivalent which must attach to the spindle. If DYNLT3 (from the maternal X) assists the dynein motors at kinetochores, it might preferentially stabilize the attachment of the X chromosome’s kinetochore to spindle microtubules. The Y, lacking DYNLT3 gene expression (due to X inactivation in meiosis), might experience a slight deficit in motor recruitment. This could manifest as subtle misalignment or tension differences – possibly increasing the chance that the Y laggs or mis-segregates. In extreme scenarios, Y chromosome lagging could result in sperm with aneuploidies or eliminated Y-bearing cells. Even a minor inefficiency could reduce Y-sperm output or quality. Another post-meiotic effect: DYNLT3 in spermatids might contribute to organizing the perinuclear cytoskeleton (since dynein and microtubules are involved in nuclear shaping). X-bearing spermatids with active DYNLT3 might more effectively elongate and shed excess cytoplasm. Y-bearing spermatids might complete these processes less efficiently, perhaps leading to slightly lower motility or increased morphologic defects in Y-sperm. All these effects would skew fertility toward X-sperm. It’s notable that Tctex-type dynein light chains have been implicated in the classic t-haplotype meiotic drive system of mice (the Tctex-3 gene is a distorter in the t-complex). DYNLT3’s human homolog resides on X, so it could represent an independent element with similar properties.
Supporting Literature: Lo et al. (2007) demonstrated that DYNLT3 directly links dynein to Bub3 at kinetochores, promoting proper chromosome congression . In mouse oocytes, knockdown of Dnylt3 caused misaligned chromosomes and segregation errors . This indicates that inadequate DYNLT3 impairs meiotic fidelity. While no human studies directly link DYNLT3 to skewed sex ratios, the requirement of dynein-dynlt3 for spindle function suggests an X advantage: the X chromosome’s presence correlates with having the DYNLT3 gene product available locally. If any asymmetry in motor function exists, it could underlie a subtle X-drive. Given the homology to mouse Tctex distorters, DYNLT3 is a plausible candidate for involvement in segregation distortion mechanisms .

title: EDA (Ectodysplasin A)
content:
Function: EDA encodes a secreted signaling protein (a TNF-family ligand) crucial for embryonic development of ectodermal appendages (teeth, hair, sweat glands). It binds the EDAR receptor to activate NF-κB signaling in ectodermal cells. In development, EDA/EDAR signaling specifies the formation of hair follicles, teeth, and glands. Mutations in EDA cause X-linked hypohidrotic ectodermal dysplasia, characterized by sparse hair, missing teeth, and lack of sweat glands. Beyond development, EDA has roles in adult tissue homeostasis (e.g., skin repair). It is not a classical “meiotic” protein.
Expression: EDA is primarily expressed in fetal ectodermal tissues and certain adult epithelia. Its expression in the testis or germline is low to negligible – the testis is not an ectodermal structure, and adult EDA mRNA levels in testis are very low (RNA-seq shows only \~1 nTPM in testis) . There is some evidence of EDA pathway activity in the epididymis or accessory glands; for instance, EDA is expressed at a slightly higher level in the epididymis (\~3 nTPM) than in testis . However, the EDAR receptor is not known to be significant in spermatogenesis.
Plausible Drive Mechanism: Given EDA’s primary role in developmental signaling rather than germ cell biology, it’s challenging to assign it a direct meiotic drive mechanism. One speculative angle is via indirect effects on the reproductive tract environment. For example, if EDA were expressed in the epididymis or seminal fluid-producing glands, it could influence the maturation conditions that differentially affect X vs Y sperm. However, there is no evidence that EDA provides a competitive advantage to X-bearing sperm. Another possibility is a systemic effect: males with hyperactive EDA signaling might have subtly altered testicular function or hormone levels, which could skew sex ratios (some signaling pathways bias X- or Y-sperm fertilization odds). Yet, no such phenomenon is documented for EDA. In fact, men with EDA mutations (who lack functional ectodysplasin) do not show skewed offspring sex ratios – their main issues lie in ectodermal structures, not fertility. Therefore, if EDA were assumed to be a “functional meiotic driver,” it would have to be via an unknown mechanism such as post-meiotic gamete selection: perhaps X-bearing sperm could benefit from a factor in seminal plasma regulated by EDA. EDA might, for instance, promote an optimal environment in the female reproductive tract (since EDA can be involved in mucosal gland development). If X-sperm were more robust in that environment, that could be a form of drive. This remains highly hypothetical.
Supporting Literature: There is scant literature connecting EDA to spermatogenesis or fertility. The Human Protein Atlas confirms that EDA expression is essentially absent in testicular germ cells . MedlinePlus and genetic reviews of ectodermal dysplasia note that while EDA mutations cause striking ectodermal defects, they do not commonly report infertility or sex ratio distortion in carriers . In summary, EDA is probably not a true meiotic driver in the germline context. Any effect on male fertility or offspring sex would be indirect, and current evidence does not support a significant role in meiotic drive.

title: EIF1AX (Eukaryotic Translation Initiation Factor 1A, X-Linked)
content:
Function: EIF1AX encodes a core factor of the 43S pre-initiation complex in translation. It is the X-linked isoform of eIF1A (distinguished from a Y-linked pseudogene/variant in some species). EIF1AX is essential for guiding the small ribosomal subunit to mRNA and scanning for the start codon. It stabilizes initiator tRNA-Met binding and ensures accurate start-site selection. In all cells, eIF1A is required for general protein synthesis and cell viability. Importantly, human EIF1AX and its Y-homolog (EIF1AY) are almost identical in function, with minor regulatory differences: EIF1AX contains an miR-1 microRNA binding site in its 3’UTR that EIF1AY lacks, causing some tissue-specific expression differences .
Expression: EIF1AX is expressed in essentially all tissues (housekeeping), including testes. Both males and females need EIF1A function, so females express EIF1AX from the active X (it escapes complete X-inactivation to meet dosage requirements), and males use EIF1AX (on their single X) as well as EIF1AY. In males, studies show EIF1AY and EIF1AX are expressed at similar levels in most tissues, including likely the testis . During spermatogenesis, there is evidence that EIF1AY is expressed in early germ cells but, like many Y genes, is inactivated during meiosis; EIF1AX may then be expressed in later stages (spermatids) when the X is reactivated. Overall, testis expresses both isoforms, and total eIF1A protein remains abundant throughout germ-cell development.
Plausible Drive Mechanism: If any meiotic drive exists for EIF1AX, it would revolve around differences in protein dosage or timing between X-bearing and Y-bearing cells. One hypothesis is haploid phase competition: in round spermatids, an X-bearing cell has an active EIF1AX gene and could produce fresh eIF1A protein, whereas a Y-bearing spermatid must rely on the residual EIF1AY produced before meiosis (since the Y can be transcribed again post-meiotically, but it might be less efficiently expressed). Because eIF1A levels modulate translational capacity, X-bearing spermatids might sustain protein synthesis slightly better during the dramatic cell remodeling of spermiogenesis. This could confer a quality advantage (e.g., more complete protamine translation or better stress resilience), making X-sperm more robust. Additionally, recent comparative analyses found that while EIF1AX and EIF1AY are usually co-expressed, in certain tissues the Y gene is expressed higher due to loss of a microRNA site . In testis, we don’t have evidence of a large imbalance; however, one could speculate that during specific windows (e.g., post-meiotic genome reactivation), the X-linked EIF1AX might respond faster than the Y gene. This subtle timing difference could skew the competitive ability of the developing sperm. In sum, any drive mechanism for EIF1AX would be post-meiotic selection via protein dosage – giving X-sperm a slight translational edge leading to higher viability or motility. It’s worth noting that due to shared cytoplasm through much of spermiogenesis, complete disparity is mitigated. Also, male mice lacking EIF1AY (in Y\^− males) are infertile, underscoring that at least one eIF1A must be present in spermatogenesis. But since EIF1AX is universally required and not known to vary in activity between alleles, its involvement in drive would be marginal.
Supporting Literature: Godfrey et al. (2020) examined X vs Y gene expression and noted EIF1AX/EIF1AY are broadly similar in expression, with some tissues favoring EIF1AY due to miRNA regulation . In the testis, both paralogs are expressed, and knockout studies in mice have not reported skewed sex ratios or competition effects (Up to now, Eif1ax mutations are mostly studied in cancers or mosaic developmental disorders, not germline drive). Given that EIF1AX is indispensable for cell viability, any strong distortion would likely be deleterious. Therefore, while conceptually an X-sperm might capitalize on its active EIF1AX gene, in practice EIF1AX is not considered a driver with significant impact.

title: EMD (Emerin)
content:
Function: EMD (Emerin) encodes a nuclear inner-membrane protein, a member of the LEM-domain family. Emerin interacts with nuclear lamina components (lamins A/C, BAF) and helps maintain nuclear architecture and mechanical integrity. It is involved in chromatin tethering to the nuclear periphery and regulation of gene expression. Mutations in EMD cause X-linked Emery-Dreifuss muscular dystrophy, illustrating its role in muscle cell nuclear structure. In germ cells, emerin (and related LEM proteins) are thought to contribute to meiotic nuclear envelope reorganization. Notably, during meiosis prophase, telomeres attach to the nuclear envelope (“bouquet” formation) and LEM-domain proteins are implicated in this process . Emerin could facilitate proper pairing by anchoring telomeres via the LINC complex.
Expression: Emerin is expressed in nearly all cells (since every nucleus has a lamina). In testis, emerin is present in Sertoli cells and all germ cells up to spermatids. Interestingly, a Drosophila emerin homolog (Otefin) is specifically expressed in germ cell nuclei and localizes to a structure called the “dense body” during meiosis. SCML1 (a protein in dense bodies) has been found co-localizing with emerin in mouse spermatocytes . Although Emd transcript is not enriched in testis, the protein is certainly there, given its fundamental role. In late spermatids, the nuclear envelope is drastically remodeled and most nuclear membrane proteins (including emerin) are lost during sperm maturation, but until then emerin plays a structural role.
Plausible Drive Mechanism: Emerin’s role in meiotic drive would likely relate to its function in meiotic chromosome segregation or post-meiotic nuclear quality. One hypothesis: preferential gamete survival via nuclear stability. During the physical stresses of spermatid elongation and chromatin compaction, nuclei must withstand mechanical forces. X-bearing spermatids produce emerin (from the active X after meiosis), which might strengthen their nuclear envelope. Y-bearing spermatids, lacking an emerin gene, must make do with whatever emerin protein was synthesized earlier and shared. If that supply is insufficient or if renewal is needed during spermiogenesis, Y-sperm nuclei could be more prone to damage or abnormal shape. For example, slight membrane fragility might lead to DNA damage (and apoptosis) in Y spermatids, or suboptimal head morphology affecting fertilization. Supporting this idea, Drosophila otefin (emerin) mutant males have been shown to gradually lose germline stem cells and have reduced fertility, a phenotype rescued by blocking DNA damage checkpoint activation . This indicates that without Otefin, germ cell nuclei accumulate damage and are culled. Similarly, X-bearing germ cells with emerin might better resist DNA damage in meiosis, whereas Y-bearing cells might activate checkpoints more often and be eliminated. Additionally, emerin is involved in meiotic sex chromosome inactivation (MSCI): it helps recruit repressive complexes to the sex chromosomes. If emerin were absent, perhaps sex chromosomes (especially the Y) experience improper silencing and meiotic arrest. X-bearing spermatocytes, having emerin, could more effectively silence the XY pair and progress through meiosis. (We note mice lacking emerin are viable, so any effect is mild or redundant.) Finally, emergent evidence in mammals shows many NE proteins are crucial for fertility . Emerin’s subtle advantage to X could thus manifest as higher yield of mature X-sperm.
Supporting Literature: A 2019 review by Pereira et al. highlights that LEM-domain NE proteins (like emerin) are involved in mammalian meiosis and spermiogenesis . In Drosophila, Otefin (emerin homolog) is required for germline maintenance; otefin mutant males exhibit premature germ cell loss and reduced fertility unless a DNA damage checkpoint is disabled . This implies emerin protects germ cells from stress. Moreover, Nature Communications (2018) showed that otefin mutant male flies have significantly decreased fertility by 2 weeks of age (\~70% of mutant males sterile vs. only \~30% of wild-type fertile) – indicating a time-dependent disadvantage for sperm lacking emerin. Translating this, one could argue X-linked emerin provides longevity and stability to the germline that Y-lacking cells cannot fully match. While humans with EMD mutations (Emery-Dreifuss patients) can be fertile (their main issues are in muscle), some case reports note testicular fibrosis or atrophy in severe cases (possibly secondary to muscle disease). Overall, emerin’s influence on drive is plausible but would be considered a minor post-meiotic selection factor favoring X.

title: HTR2C (5-Hydroxytryptamine Receptor 2C)
content:
Function: HTR2C is a serotonin (5-HT) receptor, a G protein-coupled receptor (GPCR) predominantly found in the central nervous system. It modulates neurotransmission – notably appetite, mood, and sexual behavior. HTR2C activation in the brain can induce satiety and affect anxiety. Importantly, 5-HT₂C receptors also influence male sexual function: activation of 5-HT₂C in rats increases penile erection and prolongs ejaculatory latency . Outside the brain, 5-HT₂C is expressed at lower levels in peripheral tissues. Intriguingly, evidence has emerged that components of the serotonin system (including receptors) exist in spermatozoa. Serotonin can bind sperm and affect their function; for instance, 5-HT stimulates sperm motility and the acrosome reaction in various species . While initial studies identified 5-HT₂A and 5-HT₃ on rodent sperm, rabbit sperm have shown binding consistent with a 5-HT₂-family receptor on the flagellum . Given HTR2C is the only 5-HT₂ receptor encoded on X, it’s plausible that some sperm express HTR2C.
Expression: HTR2C is highly expressed in brain regions (notably choroid plexus and limbic areas). In reproductive context, HTR2C mRNA is not abundant in testis overall. However, the protein may be present on certain cells: immunohistochemical studies of human testis are lacking, but rodent studies find 5-HT₂ receptors on sperm as mentioned. There is also evidence for serotonin production and receptors in male reproductive tracts: serotonin is found in semen, and the epididymis synthesizes 5-HT which correlates with male fertility metrics . The HTR2C gene undergoes RNA editing generating multiple isoforms (which can alter signaling efficacy). This fine-tuning suggests physiological importance, possibly even in gametes if expressed.
Plausible Drive Mechanism: If HTR2C is functional in sperm, it could mediate a post-meiotic selection mechanism. Specifically, X-bearing sperm would possess the HTR2C receptor on their surface (translated from the X gene during spermiogenesis), whereas Y-bearing sperm would lack it. Upon ejaculation into the female tract, both 5-HT from semen and possibly female tract 5-HT could engage these receptors. What’s the effect? Serotonin signaling in sperm can enhance motility and induce capacitation/acrosome reaction – changes required for fertilization. An X-sperm with HTR2C could respond to 5-HT by becoming hyperactivated or undergoing the acrosome reaction at the optimal time, whereas a Y-sperm without HTR2C might not get that stimulatory cue. Over many sperm, this could bias which ones reach or penetrate the egg. In essence, HTR2C might act as a “cheater” allele by giving X-sperm a head start in the fertilization race through serotonin-induced motility enhancement. Another angle: within the male tract, HTR2C in testicular or epididymal cells could differentially nurture X vs Y sperm. However, since sperm are mixed, that’s less likely. The most intriguing scenario remains differential sperm function in the female tract. If females produce any serotonin gradient (for example, platelets in uterine fluid release 5-HT during copulation injury), X-sperm could preferentially capitalize on it. Lastly, HTR2C’s known role in delaying ejaculation means that if a male’s HTR2C is overactive (say due to certain alleles), he might have longer copulation and larger ejaculate volumes, potentially influencing sperm competition dynamics. An allele that biases sex ratio toward X might also subtly alter mating behavior to favor its transmission. While speculative, these are conceivable routes of drive.
Supporting Literature: Serotonergic modulation of sperm is documented: Rodriguez et al. (2011) found serotonin receptors including 5-HT₂ subtypes on sperm and showed that 5-HT increases human sperm motility and tyrosine phosphorylation (a capacitation marker) . They also cited earlier work detecting 5-HT₂-like binding in sperm flagella . Meanwhile, Motofei et al. (2008) demonstrated 5-HT₂C agonists affect male sexual reflexes, highlighting a link between HTR2C and reproductive timing . Though HTR2C’s direct expression on sperm requires further confirmation, the concept of X-linked sperm-specific receptors conferring advantage is a recognized model in evolutionary biology (analogous to the t-haplotype spermicide system in mice, though that involves a distorter that kills Y-sperm). In summary, if HTR2C is indeed present on X-bearing sperm, it represents a mechanism for chemical favoritism in fertilization .

title: HUWE1 (HECT, UBA, WWE-domain E3 Ubiquitin Ligase 1)
content:
Function: HUWE1 is a very large E3 ubiquitin ligase (∼4374 amino acids) that tags specific substrate proteins with ubiquitin, marking them for degradation or functional modulation. It is a master regulator in many pathways: it can ubiquitylate the tumor suppressor p53, the replication licensing factor Cdc6, the anti-apoptotic Mcl1, histones, and multiple DNA repair factors . Of particular interest to spermatogenesis, HUWE1 (also known as ARF-BP1 or Mule) mediates the degradation of histone proteins during spermiogenesis . As spermatids condense their chromatin, HUWE1 helps remove histones (for instance by ubiquitinating H2A/H2AX) to allow protamine packaging . HUWE1 also orchestrates DNA damage responses in germ cells and is required for proper spermatogonial differentiation by controlling the balance of pro-apoptotic and repair proteins .
Expression: HUWE1 is ubiquitously expressed (consistent with its diverse roles in cell cycle and stress response), but it is highly expressed in the testis. In fact, during spermatogenesis its expression pattern is dynamic: UPF3B (discussed below) is silenced in meiosis, whereas Huwe1 stays active. HUWE1 protein is present in spermatogonia, with expression peaking as spermatocytes transition to spermatids when histone-to-protamine exchange occurs . Knockout studies in mice have shown that loss of HUWE1 in prospermatogonia or differentiating spermatogonia leads to DNA damage accumulation and apoptosis of those germ cells . Thus, HUWE1 is essential for male germ cell development (though interestingly, HUWE1-null male mice are embryonic lethal, precluding a direct fertility test; conditional mutants confirm its role in germ cells).
Plausible Drive Mechanism: HUWE1 could drive distortion at multiple levels: (1) Gamete development quality: As an X-linked gene, HUWE1 is expressed from the single X in males. There is no Y homolog, although autosomal modifiers exist (e.g., HUWE1 paralog PHF8 is separate but not redundant). If an X-bearing germ cell carries HUWE1, it will produce the ligase in pre-meiotic and post-meiotic stages. A Y-bearing germ cell cannot produce HUWE1 and must rely on maternal HUWE1 mRNA/protein until those are depleted. Critically, when spermatids need to remove histones and repair DNA breaks during chromatin reorganization, X-bearing spermatids can synthesize HUWE1 protein to ubiquitinate histones (like H2A/H2AX) and facilitate their replacement . Y-bearing spermatids, lacking HUWE1 gene, might have slower or incomplete histone removal, leading to suboptimal chromatin packaging or residual DNA damage. This could reduce Y-sperm’s integrity or motility. (2) Segregation bias: During meiosis, HUWE1’s role in DNA damage checkpoints might differentially affect X vs Y. Studies show that Huwe1-deficient spermatogonia experience a heightened DNA damage response and are eliminated . The sex chromosomes endure heavy DNA double-strand breaks during recombination; an absence of HUWE1 might trigger checkpoint-mediated elimination of those spermatocytes. If HUWE1 were absent (as in a Y-bearing spermatocyte where HUWE1 might be mostly silenced due to MSCI and lack of a backup copy), perhaps it is less efficient at resolving sex chromatin DNA lesions, invoking cell death pathways. In contrast, an X-bearing spermatocyte (with HUWE1 escaping MSCI to some extent or acting before MSCI) could better manage and survive. This scenario aligns with the known antagonistic paralog relationship between HUWE1 and its putative X-escaping suppressor gene USP26 in some models, though in reality HUWE1 itself likely is subject to MSCI and would act primarily pre-meiotically. All told, the most tangible advantage is in the haploid phase: X-sperm benefiting from HUWE1 in finishing spermiogenesis properly (e.g., eliminating damaged proteins and ensuring high-quality sperm). Over many generations, this could drive the HUWE1 allele to persist.
Supporting Literature: Liu et al. (2007) first identified HUWE1 (as E3\^Histone) as the ligase that ubiquitinates histone H2A during spermatid elongation . They found Huwe1 expression is strongly upregulated in late spermatids, coinciding with histone removal . More recently, Bose et al. (2017) and Fok et al. (2017) showed that loss of Huwe1 in mouse spermatogonia led to accumulation of γH2AX (marker of DNA damage) and apoptosis of those cells . These studies collectively underscore HUWE1’s importance in germ cell genomic integrity and differentiation. Furthermore, a 2022 review by Zhang et al. notes that HUWE1 is a “giant E3” often having dosage-sensitive effects and has been linked to intellectual disability and inflammatory disease when mutated . In terms of drive, one can draw parallels to the Segregation Distorter (SD) system in fruit flies, where a locus that affects chromatin/DNA integrity in spermatids causes dysfunction of sperm lacking it. HUWE1 might represent a similar principle: X-bearing sperm fully execute sperm chromatin maturation, whereas Y-bearing sperm (without HUWE1 in late phase) might suffer a mild “immaturity” handicap. Notably, male germ cells have a backup in the form of autosomal Upf3a during meiosis (for NMD) but for ubiquitin ligation of histones, HUWE1 is unique on X. Given HUWE1’s central role in sperm development, it is one of the more convincing candidates for an X-linked factor that could enhance X-sperm fitness .

title: IQSEC2 (IQ Motif and Sec7 Domain 2, aka BRAG1)
content:
Function: IQSEC2 is a guanine nucleotide exchange factor (GEF) for the ARF family of small GTPases, specifically ARF6. It contains a Sec7 domain that catalyzes the exchange of GDP for GTP on ARF6, activating it. ARF6 regulates membrane dynamics at the cell periphery, including endosome recycling, actin remodeling, and vesicle trafficking. In neurons, IQSEC2/BRAG1 is postsynaptic and helps mediate AMPA receptor trafficking (mutations cause intellectual disability and epilepsy). The protein also has IQ motifs (calmodulin-binding) linking Ca²⁺ signals to ARF6 activation. In summary, IQSEC2 is a signaling hub that controls membrane remodeling events in response to cellular signals.
Expression: IQSEC2 is highly expressed in the brain (particularly cortex and hippocampus) and was thought to be brain-specific (hence “BRAG” – Brain ARF-GEF). Nevertheless, transcripts are present in other tissues at lower levels . Single-cell RNA-seq indicates Iqsec2 is expressed during mouse spermatocyte stages, similar to the expression pattern of recombination protein DMC1 . This suggests IQSEC2 might be upregulated in mid-meiosis. Also, ARF6 – the target GTPase – is known to function in germ cells: ARF6 is present in human sperm (localized to the acrosomal region) and is essential for the acrosome reaction, as it coordinates membrane fusion and calcium efflux during exocytosis . Thus, if IQSEC2 is expressed in spermatids or sperm, it could regulate ARF6 in that context. There is no Y-homolog; IQSEC2 is one of a kind on the X, although other ARF-GEFs (like cytohesins or EFA6 family) are autosomal and could be in germ cells too.
Plausible Drive Mechanism: Post-meiotic functional advantage via membrane trafficking. If IQSEC2 protein is present in developing spermatids (especially in the phase of acrosome formation and flagellum construction), an X-bearing spermatid would have this ARF6-GEF to assist in intracellular trafficking. ARF6 and its effectors regulate processes such as acrosomal vesicle fusion and organization of the sperm head membrane. An IQSEC2-proficient (X) spermatid might assemble the acrosome more faithfully or ensure proper distribution of membrane proteins on the sperm surface. In contrast, a Y-bearing spermatid lacks IQSEC2 and might rely on slower or less coordinated pathways for the same tasks, possibly leading to a fraction of Y-sperm with defective acrosomes or weaker capacitation response. Additionally, during fertilization, ARF6 in sperm must become activated to facilitate acrosome reaction upon encountering the egg’s zona pellucida . X-sperm carrying IQSEC2 could potentially activate ARF6 faster or more robustly (e.g., if residual IQSEC2 protein remains in mature sperm), thus undergoing acrosome reaction efficiently and penetrating the egg coat sooner. Y-sperm would need to activate ARF6 through baseline, perhaps slower mechanisms. This kinetic edge for X-sperm at the moment of fertilization could bias successful fertilization events toward X-chromosome bearers. Finally, ARF6 is also present in intercellular bridges between germ cells ; one study found that ARF6 is a component of the stable intercellular bridges in male germ cells and that Arf6 knockout in mice led to disrupted cytokinesis in spermatocytes . IQSEC2 in X-bearing cells might stabilize ARF6 function in bridges, helping germ cell syncytium communication. If Y-bearing cells have less ARF6 activation in bridges, they might suffer more from fragmentation or loss of cytoplasmic sharing, possibly impacting their development. This is speculative but illustrates how an ARF-GEF on X could influence germ cell viability in subtle ways.
Supporting Literature: ARF6’s role in sperm exocytosis is well documented: for instance, J. Biol. Chem. (2015) reported that active ARF6 triggers acrosomal membrane fusion, and adding an ARF6 activator can induce the acrosome reaction . They also note ARF6 requires upstream GEFs (like EFA6 or potentially BRAG family) to become activated in sperm . All ARF6 GEF families (EFA6, cytohesin, BRAG) have the capability to activate ARF6 , but IQSEC2 (BRAG1) is unique in being X-linked. Evolutionarily, genes affecting sperm-egg interaction often show signs of positive selection; indeed, IQSEC2 has undergone adaptive evolution in primates (though mostly discussed for brain, its testis role is unclear) . Clinically, IQSEC2 mutations cause neurological disorders, not infertility, which implies no absolute requirement for spermatogenesis. However, the presence of IQSEC2 in spermatogenic cells and the critical function of ARF6 in acrosome reaction support a model where IQSEC2 could make X-sperm more “fit” for fertilization.

title: SPANXN5 (Sperm Protein Associated with Nucleus, X-linked, family N, member 5)
content:
Function: SPANX proteins are a family of cancer/testis antigens found in post-meiotic male germ cells. They are named for their localization: associated with the nucleus in sperm (particularly the nuclear envelope or perinuclear region). The SPANX-N subfamily (including SPANXN1–N5) is present in primates and rodents, whereas the SPANX-A/D subfamily is hominid-specific . Although the precise molecular function is not fully elucidated, recent proteomic analysis indicates SPANX proteins play roles in nuclear envelope organization, energy metabolism, and flagellar function in sperm . SPANX-A/D proteins bind to components of the nuclear pore and metabolic enzymes like fumarate hydratase, suggesting they help organize the tightly compacted sperm nucleus and possibly link it to the flagellum for coordinated movement . They have also been implicated in chromatin packaging and may ensure the sperm nuclear envelope is properly remodeled and sealed after histone-protamine transition.
Expression: SPANXN5, like other SPANX-N members, is expressed exclusively in the testis, specifically in late spermatids and in mature spermatozoa . All SPANX family members appear for the first time in haploid cells (spermatids) and are not detected in earlier stages . SPANX-N proteins localize to a unique aspect of sperm nuclear architecture: for example, some SPANX proteins cap the posterior pole of the sperm nucleus (where the tail inserts) and are thought to help tether the tail cytoskeleton to the nucleus. Others are found around the nucleus beneath the nuclear envelope. In human ejaculated sperm, SPANX-A/D isoforms are present and can be phosphorylated, indicating functionality . SPANXN5’s exact localization is not separately reported, but by homology it likely behaves like other SPANX-N (e.g., SPANXN1–4 are roughly 99% identical to each other). This means SPANXN5 is a post-meiotic, sperm-specific protein.
Plausible Drive Mechanism: SPANXN5 provides a clear scenario for X-linked meiotic drive via sperm competition. Because SPANX-N proteins are only made in haploid cells and cannot diffuse between sister spermatids (they associate with nucleus or insoluble structures), an X-bearing spermatid will have SPANXN5 protein whereas a Y-bearing one will not. The advantage may come in several forms: (1) Nuclear integrity: SPANX proteins at the nuclear envelope may ensure the nucleus attains its proper shape and that nuclear pores are correctly closed off as chromatin condenses. Y-sperm lacking SPANX might have slight abnormalities, such as minor envelope disruptions or less stable attachment of the tail to the head (which could affect motility). (2) Metabolic boost: The interaction of SPANX with metabolic enzymes suggests X-sperm might better localize or regulate enzymes that produce energy for flagellar movement. For instance, SPANX-A/D’s interaction with fumarate hydratase ties sperm motility to efficient Krebs cycle flux . If SPANXN5 plays a similar role, X-bearing sperm could have a higher ATP supply in the flagellum, translating to faster swimming. (3) Flagellar coordination: SPANX family proteins also interact with axonemal components like Tektin-1 . This could mean X-sperm maintain more synchronized or robust flagellar beating. Taken together, X-bearing sperm would outcompete Y-bearing sperm in reaching and penetrating the egg. Over generations, this bias allows the X-linked SPANXN5 gene to propagate (even if it might slightly reduce overall male fertility, it biases towards female offspring carrying the allele). It’s essentially a classic “sex-ratio distorter” model: SPANXN5 makes X-sperm hardier or more motile than their Y counterparts, skewing the sex ratio of offspring toward females. Importantly, since SPANX proteins are not required for fertilization per se (mice lacking the analogous Spanx genes are still fertile, suggesting redundancy or subtlety), the effect is not absolute but probabilistic – enough to tilt odds without eliminating Y-sperm entirely.
Supporting Literature: Urizar-Arenaza et al. (2020) demonstrated the multifaceted roles of SPANX-A/D in human sperm, highlighting their involvement in nuclear envelope integrity, metabolism (fumarate hydratase interaction), and flagellar structure (Tektin 1 interaction) . They conclude that SPANX proteins are “multifunctional” in sperm physiology and are “key” for sperm structure and motility . Evolutionarily, the SPANX-N genes (like SPANXN5) have expanded and diverged under positive selection in primates , which is often a signature of reproductive conflict or competition. Moreover, SPANX genes are located on the X and expressed only after meiosis – textbook conditions for a driver: they don’t impose a handicap on female carriers (since females don’t express them except in ovaries minimally), but in males they can distort competition in favor of themselves. The adaptive diversification of SPANXN5 in primates suggests it could be engaged in an arms race, possibly with suppressors on the Y or autosomes, to modulate sex ratio. While direct experiments on SPANXN5 specifically are lacking, the collective data on SPANX family supports its candidacy as a post-meiotic driver favoring X-bearing sperm .

title: OCRL (Inositol Polyphosphate-5-Phosphatase OCRL-1)
content:
Function: OCRL is a phosphatidylinositol 4,5-bisphosphate 5-phosphatase localized to the trans-Golgi and endosomes. It hydrolyzes PI(4,5)P₂ to PI(4)P, regulating membrane trafficking and actin dynamics. Mutations in OCRL cause Lowe syndrome (oculocerebrorenal syndrome), characterized by kidney dysfunction, cataracts, and neurological impairment. OCRL is involved in endocytic vesicle recycling and primary cilium function; it interacts with clathrin adaptor APPL1 and the endosomal actin machinery. In the context of reproduction, OCRL and its paralog INPP5B have redundant roles. Mice lacking Ocrl alone show no obvious infertility (they largely phenocopy nothing due to Inpp5b compensation), but Inpp5b-knockout male mice are infertile due to spermatogenesis defects and immotile sperm . This indicates that 5-phosphatase activity is crucial in the testis and sperm, and either OCRL or INPP5B must be present. Indeed, Ocrl–/–; Inpp5b–/– double knockout is embryonic lethal in mice , confirming their combined necessity. OCRL specifically has been implicated in organizing the actin structure of the sperm tail and in membrane trafficking during sperm maturation (by analogy to its known role in cilia, and sperm flagellum is a modified cilium).
Expression: OCRL is expressed in many tissues; in the testis, both OCRL and INPP5B transcripts are present. Notably, INPP5B (the autosomal 5-phosphatase) is highly expressed in male germ cells and partly compensates for OCRL. During spermatogenesis, OCRL is transcribed in spermatogonia and early spermatocytes (until the sex chromosomes are silenced). After meiosis, any new OCRL protein would come from the X in spermatids – but the X is reactivated only in round spermatids, so some OCRL may be produced then. The key observation is that in human tissues where OCRL is absent, INPP5B often fills in (e.g., human eyes: OCRL is in certain ocular cells while INPP5B is minimal ; in testis it’s presumably the opposite pattern: robust INPP5B, less OCRL). Interestingly, in murine testes, INPP5B is strongly expressed in germ cells and localizes to sperm flagella and acrosomes, whereas Ocrl is also present but perhaps in different compartments (some studies suggest OCRL in Sertoli cell junctions or spermatid Golgi). Inpp5b knockout males show spermatogenic failure with many multinucleated spermatids and immotile sperm . This suggests that in mice, OCRL alone cannot fully support spermatogenesis – possibly due to inadequate expression in certain cells, or because INPP5B has a unique role in late spermatids. Conversely, human patients with Lowe syndrome (OCRL loss) are not uniformly infertile, presumably because INPP5B in humans picks up the slack.
Plausible Drive Mechanism: Despite the complexity, an X-linked distortion scenario can be conceived. If OCRL is expressed even a little in post-meiotic X-bearing spermatids, it could complement the function of INPP5B for those cells, whereas Y-bearing spermatids must rely solely on INPP5B. Consider processes like removal of excess cytoplasm (cytoplasmic droplet) or construction of the acrosome and flagellum – these involve intense membrane remodeling and actin changes, which PI(4,5)P₂ signaling (OCRL’s pathway) regulates. X-bearing spermatids with an extra dose or activity of 5-phosphatase might resolve PI(4,5)P₂ more efficiently, ensuring timely vesicle uncoating and disassembly of actin structures during sperm individualization. Y-bearing ones might experience slight delays or irregularities in those steps. The outcome could be that a subset of Y-sperm are morphologically abnormal (e.g., with persistent cytoplasmic droplets or structural tail defects) and thus less competitive. Additionally, in the female tract, both OCRL and INPP5B are present in sperm; having both might improve the rapid acrosome reaction or tail hyperactivation. If X-sperm carry OCRL protein into the ejaculate in addition to INPP5B, they might respond faster to the cues for capacitation, since 5-phosphatases help modulate the PI(4,5)P₂ required for acrosome exocytosis. Y-sperm lacking OCRL would depend on INPP5B alone; interestingly, Inpp5b\^–/– sperm in mice were immotile , implying that having at least one 5-phosphatase is vital. In humans, both are usually present; but if one imagines a scenario where INPP5B levels vary, an X-sperm’s OCRL could rescue borderline cases. Therefore, the X-linked OCRL might subtly enhance sperm quality under stressful conditions (e.g., suboptimal environment), giving X-sperm a survival edge. Over evolutionary time, this dynamic can drive the persistence of OCRL and might skew offspring sex if the effect is consistent. However, since INPP5B is quite active in testis, any OCRL advantage might manifest only when INPP5B function is limiting (perhaps in older males or certain stress conditions).
Supporting Literature: Zhang et al. (2013) examined the compensatory roles of OCRL and INPP5B: they found that knockout of Inpp5b in mice caused male infertility with severe spermatogenic defects, whereas Ocrl knockout alone did not, and double KO was embryonic lethal . They also noted differences in ocular distribution of OCRL vs INPP5B which explain why mice lacking Ocrl don’t have Lowe syndrome phenotypes (the redundancy in eyes) . Extrapolating to testis, their data and others’ (e.g., Jänne’s earlier work) suggest INPP5B is the dominant player in mouse spermatids , but in human spermatids, OCRL might have a bit more role, as human INPP5B doesn’t fully compensate Lowe syndrome in other tissues. Additionally, experiments on sperm from Inpp5b KO mice showed immotility and malformed heads, linking PI(4,5)P₂ misregulation to flagellar and head defects. These findings collectively hint that the presence of two functional 5-phosphatases in spermatids (as X-bearing cells have) is superior to one (Y-bearing). Thus, while OCRL’s drive effect may be mild, it aligns with the idea of X-linked factors ensuring more robust sperm formation – a form of genetic altruism benefiting the X chromosome’s propagation.

title: PHF8 (PHD Finger Protein 8)
content:
Function: PHF8 is a chromatin-modifying enzyme – specifically, a JmjC domain histone demethylase. It recognizes trimethylated lysine-4 on histone H3 (H3K4me3, a mark of active promoters) via its PHD finger and demethylates repressive histone marks such as H3K9me2, H3K27me2, and H4K20me1 . By removing these methyl groups, PHF8 activates transcription of genes. It has roles in cell cycle progression (through demethylation of CDK1 and CCNA2 gene promoters), neural development, and craniofacial formation. Mutations in PHF8 cause X-linked intellectual disability with cleft lip (Siderius syndrome), underscoring its developmental importance. In germ cells, histone methylation dynamics are crucial during meiosis and the post-meiotic transition – large-scale changes in H3K9 and H3K27 methylation accompany meiosis and spermiogenesis. PHF8, being an H3K9me2 demethylase, likely contributes to gene reactivation after meiotic sex chromosome inactivation or activation of spermatid-specific genes. Notably, PHF8 is one of few histone demethylases on the X, which might allow it to escape MSCI and function on the sex chromatin in spermatocytes.
Expression: PHF8 is widely expressed but particularly high in brain and testis. Proteomic surveys have identified PHF8 in human sperm nuclei – indicating that some PHF8 protein made in late spermatogenesis remains in mature sperm. During spermatogenesis, single-cell data show PHF8 mRNA is upregulated in pachytene spermatocytes (similar timing as DMC1 expression) . This suggests PHF8 could be active at the pachytene stage to remove repressive marks and enable transcription of late-meiosis genes or facilitate the massive chromatin reorganization after recombination. It likely continues to be expressed in round spermatids, where large swathes of the genome (including the X) are reactivated for post-meiotic expression. In round spermatids, removal of H3K9me2/H4K20me1 by PHF8 could be critical for activating haploid-specific genes and marking certain genomic regions for eventual retention of specific histones or protamine incorporation. Because PHF8 escapes X-inactivation (like many genes in the Xp11 region), it might be transcribed from the X even while other genes are silenced, providing it a unique temporal window of action.
Plausible Drive Mechanism: Transcriptional robustness in X-bearing spermatids is the likely mechanism. X-bearing spermatids have the PHF8 gene and can produce PHF8 enzyme in situ; Y-bearing spermatids cannot. PHF8 in an X-spermatid would actively demethylate H3K9me2 and related marks on autosomes and the X chromatin, thereby promoting a more open chromatin state conducive to necessary gene expression. Key spermiogenesis genes (for chromatin condensation, flagellum formation, etc.) might be more efficiently expressed in X-bearing spermatids due to PHF8 action. In Y-bearing spermatids, persistence of repressive methylation (from lack of PHF8) could lead to suboptimal or delayed gene expression. For example, if protamine genes or TNP (transition protein) genes have residual H3K9me2, their expression might be slower, resulting in a delay or inefficiency in chromatin packaging for Y-sperm. This could cause minor DNA damage or less condensed DNA in Y-sperm, ultimately affecting their function or lifespan. Another stage is during meiosis: PHF8 in X-bearing spermatocytes might help resolve MSCI by demethylating heterochromatic marks on the X once pachytene is over, ensuring that the X chromosome’s active genes re-engage properly in round spermatids. Y-spermatocytes lack PHF8 and might be slower to reactivate the Y (which has few genes but still needs some, like Zfy2, expressed). If reactivation is mis-timed, it can trigger spermatid apoptotic checkpoints. Therefore, PHF8 could give X-germ cells a smoother transition through the post-meiotic program. Summing up, PHF8 would act as an X-linked enhancer of germ cell epigenetic reprogramming – not absolutely required (since PHF8-null men have normal gametogenesis aside from being rare due to syndromic issues), but conferring a competitive edge. The result: sperm derived from PHF8-proficient spermatids (i.e., X-bearing) might be more chromatin-stable and functionally competent, biasing fertilization success.
Supporting Literature: An MDPI Genes 2021 article by Qi et al. highlighted the prominent expression of PHF8 in testis and noted that histone demethylases are crucial for spermatogenesis . It specifically mentions PHF8’s involvement in removing H3K9me2 and H4K20me1 – marks that are associated with condensed, inactive chromatin – which must be erased for proper gene activation in late spermatogenesis . Furthermore, proteomic analysis by Liu et al. (2014) detected PHF8 in normozoospermic human sperm, suggesting that PHF8 might set up an epigenetic state in sperm that could even influence the embryo (though that’s beyond drive, it implies its presence is normal in sperm) . Genetic observations show that Phf8 knockout male mice were subfertile with smaller litter sizes (though detailed studies are sparse, anecdotal reports exist in mouse model discussions), which is consistent with slightly less competitive sperm. Given that PHF8’s demethylase activity can fine-tune the expression of many spermatogenic genes, its absence could cause minor spermiogenic defects that preferentially afflict Y-sperm. In the evolutionary context, PHF8 is under constraint because loss causes a syndrome, but any allele enhancing its expression in spermatids might be positively selected if it skews sex ratio to favor X (and mothers of those daughters then pass on the allele). PHF8 stands out as a plausible meiotic drive factor because it ties directly into epigenetic reprogramming unique to haploid males – an elegant way for the X to “wire in” an advantage at the chromatin level.

title: PRICKLE3 (Prickle Planar Cell Polarity Protein 3)
content:
Function: PRICKLE3 is a lesser-known member of the Prickle family, involved in the planar cell polarity (PCP) pathway. PCP proteins organize the cytoskeleton across cells to align structures (e.g., orientation of hairs, polarized cell arrangements). Prickles typically bind core PCP proteins like Vangl and Dishevelled at cell membranes to transmit polarity cues. PRICKLE3 (also called LMO6) has not been deeply characterized; it shares some homology with PRICKLE1/2 but is more divergent and might have partially distinct roles. Notably, PCP proteins in the testis contribute to oriented cell divisions and polarized organization of elongating spermatids within the seminiferous epithelium . For example, PRICKLE1 in Sertoli cells recruits proteins to the site of attachment with elongating spermatids, affecting cytoskeletal anchoring necessary for sperm release . By analogy, PRICKLE3 might be expressed in either germ cells or supporting cells to assist in similar processes. Some evidence suggests Prickle3 can form apical PCP complexes with Par3 in epithelial cells , which could translate to germ cell epithelia like the seminiferous tubule.
Expression: Prickle3 is broadly expressed in various tissues, but within the testis, single-cell RNA data (if available) might show whether it’s in germ cells or Sertoli cells. The Human Protein Atlas indicates PRICKLE3 mRNA is detected in many tissues with low specificity, including the testis, and at the protein level, annotation suggests expression during spermatogenesis . Given PCP’s role in spermatid polarity, PRICKLE3 could be active in spermatids as they align their heads and tails in bundles coordinated by Sertoli cell microtubules. If PRICKLE3 localizes to the posterior pole of spermatid heads (where the flagellum anchors), it might help ensure proper insertion and orientation of the tail.
Plausible Drive Mechanism: If PRICKLE3 is X-linked and functional in germ cells, one could imagine it influencing the physical positioning and retention of spermatids, thereby affecting whether they mature or are prematurely lost. During the final stages, roughly half of developing spermatids are phagocytosed by Sertoli cells due to failures in remodeling – this is a quality control. If X-bearing spermatids, armed with PRICKLE3, adhere better or coordinate cytoskeleton remodeling more effectively with Sertoli cells, they might have a higher chance to survive to become sperm. Y-bearing spermatids, lacking PRICKLE3, might be slightly less synchronized, increasing the likelihood of detachment or signaling for elimination (e.g., if their orientation is off, they might be marked as defective). Additionally, PCP components like PRICKLE3 might influence the spermatocyte division plane. There is evidence PCP proteins guide oriented cell division in testes which affects which cells remain stem cells vs differentiate . If PRICKLE3 in spermatogonia biases an orientation that somehow gives an advantage to lineage carrying the X (this is speculative – perhaps an oriented division that helps X-bearing secondary spermatocytes localize in a niche with slightly better support?), that could create a subtle frequency bias. More straightforward: consider sperm structure – proper alignment of midpiece and tail is essential for motility. PCP protein malfunction can lead to structural sperm defects (like tail attachment issues). PRICKLE3 as an X factor could ensure X-sperm have perfectly aligned tails, whereas a fraction of Y-sperm might have tail angulation or skew that impedes swimming. Over many sperm, this translates to a competitive edge in motility for X-sperm.
Supporting Literature: While PRICKLE3-specific studies in reproduction are scant, general PCP research supports its putative role. A study by Cell Reports (2019) on PCP in testis found that “developing haploid spermatids utilize PCP protein complexes for organization” and that disrupting PCP leads to disorganized spermatid bundles and inefficient sperm release . Moreover, Li et al. (2015) showed that Prickle1 in Sertoli cells is required for the polarized organization of the seminiferous epithelium and that loss of Prickle1 causes disoriented spermatids and a break in the Sertoli-spermatid adhesion, resulting in infertility . By extension, an X-linked Prickle3 in germ cells might complement Sertoli PCP signals to maintain adhesion. “Adaptive evolution of SCML1 in primates” (which was actually referencing SCML1, but PRICKLE3 might similarly be under selective pressure if involved in male reproduction) hints that X genes involved in sperm development often evolve rapidly due to sperm competition . If PRICKLE3 had any role in biasing success of X-sperm, that could drive its retention on X. In summary, though direct evidence is lacking, the concept of X-linked PCP factors aligns with known mechanisms where t-haplotype distorters in mice affect sperm motility through cytoskeletal changes. PRICKLE3 could be an X analogue, subtly ensuring better structural organization for X-bearing germ cells.

title: RBM41 (RNA-Binding Motif Protein 41)
content:
Function: RBM41 is an RNA-binding protein containing an RNA-recognition motif (RRM). Its exact targets aren’t well characterized, but RBM41 is predicted to bind RNAs and possibly influence their splicing, stability or localization. Many RBM family proteins play roles in mRNA splicing regulation or translational repression. Notably, RBM41’s Y-chromosome counterpart, RBMY, is a critical spermatogenesis factor: RBMY binds pre-mRNAs in the testis to promote their splicing, and loss of all functional copies of RBMY (due to AZFb deletions) causes spermatogenic failure . While RBM41 is not simply a duplicate of RBMY (RBM41 is on Xp11, distinct from the RBMX and RBMY family in sequence), it might have analogous RNA targets in germ cells. In essence, RBM41 could be part of the network of RNA-binding proteins managing gene expression in germ cells at the post-transcriptional level.
Expression: RBM41 is broadly expressed but shows relatively high expression in testis among tissues (GTEx indicates testis as one of the top tissues for RBM41 expression) . This suggests a specialized role in germ cells. Likely, RBM41 is expressed in spermatogonia and spermatocytes (when transcription is active) and perhaps again post-meiotically (from the reactivated X in spermatids). Its protein could localize to nuclear speckles (splicing centers) or to the cytoplasmic RNA granules (chromatoid body) in spermatids. The chromatoid body is an RNP aggregate in round spermatids that controls mRNA storage and degradation, and many RNA-binding proteins (like RBM17, DDX4, etc.) reside there. It’s plausible RBM41 helps regulate a subset of transcripts during the haploid phase. For example, it might bind to protamine or other crucial mRNAs, timing their translation.
Plausible Drive Mechanism: Given that RBM41 is X-linked while a key male fertility gene RBMY is Y-linked, there could be a subtle antagonism/compensation interplay. RBMY is needed for spermatogenesis (to ensure proper splicing of testis-specific transcripts) . RBM41 might provide overlapping or complementary RNA-binding functions. In an X-bearing spermatid, you have RBM41 (from X) present, and none of the Y’s unique RBMY (Y-spermatids would have residual RBMY protein but cannot produce new RBMY after meiosis because Y is inactive, and RBMY might not be expressed postmeiotically). It’s known that many germ cell mRNAs go through alternative splicing and polyadenylation during meiosis and spermiogenesis – X-bearing cells with RBM41 might handle these RNA processing events more efficiently. For instance, consider transcripts required for sperm tail construction: if mis-spliced, the protein is faulty. RBM41 in X cells could ensure error-free splicing where Y cells, lacking this help and also having no new RBMY, might accumulate splicing errors or have reduced levels of some proteins. Additionally, RBM41 could in theory bind and stabilize certain mRNAs in spermatids. X-spermatids with RBM41 might better preserve transcripts needed later (e.g., during caput epididymal maturation when some translation still occurs in sperm). Y-sperm might be missing some of those proteins due to less stable mRNA and thus be at a disadvantage in fertilization capability. In summary, RBM41 could act as a post-meiotic mRNA quality controller more abundant in X-sperm, leading to higher quality of protein synthesis and function. A direct competition effect could also occur at the level of spermatid viability: if improper splicing triggers an apoptotic checkpoint in spermatids, Y-bearing ones might be more prone to it without RBM41. The result is a subtle skew favoring the survival of X-bearing sperm.
Supporting Literature: The importance of RNA-binding proteins for sperm development is exemplified by RBMY – an AZF gene whose absence causes spermatogenic arrest . While RBM41 is not proven to be as crucial, the testis-enriched expression suggests it has some specific germline role. A Nature Communications paper (2014) “Multiple roles for NMD revealed in male reproduction” noted that many RNA surveillance and binding factors are differentially required in spermatogenesis . Although that was about NMD, it underscores that the RNA processing environment in testis is unique and sensitive. If RBM41 binds RNAs similar to RBMY’s targets (perhaps a subset on the X or autosomes), it could complement Y’s function. Interestingly, RBMX (another X-linked RBP) is known to escape X-inactivation and act in somatic cells, possibly backing up RBMY’s role. RBM41 might be another such backup or fine-tuner. In primate evolution, RBM41 doesn’t show dramatic changes, but one could surmise that if it conferred even a small sex ratio bias, it would be maintained. Summarily, no direct experiments tie RBM41 to sex ratio distortion, but by drawing parallels to RBMY’s necessity and the general reliance of spermatids on RBPs for late gene expression, RBM41 emerges as a potential soft driver – ensuring X-sperm are transcriptionally and translationally “in tune” to outcompete Y-sperm.

title: RTL4 (Retrotransposon Gag-Like 4)
content:
Function: RTL4 is a peculiar gene – it originates from a long terminal repeat (LTR) retrotransposon of the MaLR family, but has been co-opted (“domesticated”) into a host gene. RTL4 encodes a protein with similarity to retroviral Gag polyprotein. It is one of at least 11 such Mart (MaLR retrotransposon) genes in humans . The function of RTL4 is not fully determined. Research suggests it may play roles in the brain: one study proposed RTL4 contributed to brain evolution and that it’s a candidate gene for X-linked intellectual disability . As a Gag-like protein, RTL4 might bind RNA or form higher-order protein assemblies (Gag proteins typically oligomerize to form virus-like particles). Some domesticated retroelements in other species serve in gene regulation or as scaffolds in germ cells. For instance, a mouse Mart family gene (Intracisternal A-particle retrotransposons) produces RNAs and proteins that can impact neighboring gene expression. RTL4 could similarly influence gene expression by sequestering RNAs or proteins. Intriguingly, many transposon-derived genes are expressed in the placenta or testis, where genomic conflict is pronounced. Notably, the placenta uses domesticated retroviral envelope genes for fusion (e.g., Syncytins). It’s possible that RTL4 might have a role in the placenta or embryo; however, given its mention in an evolutionary context for the brain, its testis role is speculative.
Expression: The expression profile of RTL4 is not well published. It is on the X chromosome, so expressed in both sexes but subject to X-inactivation in females. Some Mart-derived genes show male germline expression, often during meiosis or in spermatids, because the germline sometimes “reawakens” transposon expression as part of a regulatory program. The Human Protein Atlas single-cell data indicates expression in the “spermatogenesis” cluster, which may hint RTL4 transcripts are present in spermatogenic cells . If RTL4 produces a protein in testis, it might localize to germ cell cytoplasm or nuclei and form particle-like structures (similar to retroviral capsids). Those could conceivably be involved in RNA storage or piRNA pathways (since germ cells use transposon proteins in piRNA formation sometimes). In summary, RTL4 might be expressed at low levels in spermatocytes or spermatids, but this is largely hypothetical. It’s also plausible RTL4 is more active in the early embryo or placenta, which could indirectly affect offspring development in a parent-of-origin manner.
Plausible Drive Mechanism: If RTL4 were a functional meiotic driver, it would likely operate by affecting embryonic development after fertilization in a way that favors the transmission of the X that carried it. One wild notion: Subtle sabotage of Y-bearing sperm or embryos. For instance, RTL4 protein or mRNA could be deposited in sperm during spermatogenesis and then carried into the zygote. Since it’s X-linked, only X-bearing sperm (or secondary oocytes) would have RTL4 transcripts/protein to deposit. In the zygote, if RTL4 forms a complex that (for example) influences early gene activation, it might confer a slight viability advantage to XX embryos over XY embryos. This could skew sex ratios by a higher female live birth rate. Such embryonic-drive models are hard to verify but not unprecedented (there are known cases of “sex ratio distortion” due to differential embryo viability). Another scenario: in spermatogenesis itself, RTL4 might protect X-bearing spermatocytes from transposon activity or genomic stress. Retrotransposon-like proteins sometimes act as suppressors of retroelements via self-assembly. Possibly, X-bearing cells using RTL4 could better control transposon explosions during epigenetic resetting, whereas Y-bearing cells without it suffer more DNA damage or piRNA disruptions. This could lead to attrition of Y-bearing germ cells. Given the lack of direct evidence, these ideas are highly conjectural. Yet, the rapid evolution of RTL4 (as a recently domesticated gene) and its implication in cognition suggests it underwent selection, possibly due to reproductive benefits. It being on X hints at an X-specific agenda.
Supporting Literature: A J-stage review (Yohn et al., 2005) on brain evolution posited RTL4 as a candidate gene for contributing to cognitive differences, noting its presence only in eutherian mammals and potential effect on the noradrenergic system . This underscores that RTL4 has gained functionality after its retroviral origin, but details remain unknown. GeneCards mentions a disease association with inflammatory bowel disease , which might be anecdotal. Critically, the concept of a retroelement-derived gene acting in meiotic drive finds parallel in Drosophila paternally-expressed Segregation Distorter locus, which is a transposase remnant that causes dysfunction of sperm not carrying it. If RTL4 had any similar “toxin-antidote” behavior – say producing a product that is neutralized only in X-bearing gametes – that would be a direct drive. However, no studies yet show RTL4 toxic to Y-sperm. Until functional studies in germ cells are done, we must consider RTL4’s drive potential as highly theoretical. It is listed here for completeness, given the question’s assumption, but currently RTL4’s influence on male fertility or skew is not documented in the literature.

title: SCML1 (Sex Comb on Midleg-Like 1)
content:
Function: SCML1 is an X-linked polycomb group protein. Polycomb proteins generally maintain transcriptional repression through chromatin modifications. SCML1 contains a MBT (malignant brain tumor) domain that can bind methylated lysines on histones, suggesting it acts as a reader of repressive marks and part of a repressor complex . SCML1 is intriguingly expressed in male germ cells – it was found concentrated in “meiotic dense bodies” within spermatocytes . These dense bodies are sub-nuclear structures in meiosis of unknown function (discovered in rat). The recent knockout of Scml1 in mice showed that neither SCML1 nor the dense body is essential for fertility; Scml1-null males were fertile with normal gametogenesis . This suggests SCML1’s role is subtle or redundant. However, evolutionary analysis reveals SCML1 underwent adaptive evolution in primates – especially in its MBT domain – hinting at a selective pressure possibly related to reproductive function . Often, adaptively evolving genes in germline are those involved in genetic conflict or sexual selection.
Expression: SCML1 expression is largely confined to the testis (and perhaps ovary). It is predominantly expressed in spermatogonial stem cells and early spermatocytes . Immunostaining in mouse shows SCML1 protein in nuclei of spermatocytes, where it coalesces into the dense body structure in mid-pachytene . After meiosis, SCML1 is not detected in spermatids. Thus, SCML1 functions in the meiotic phase. It may associate with the sex chromosomes or other chromatin needing repression – polycomb proteins often silence developmental genes during meiosis. Notably, SCML1 is on the X, but appears to escape full MSCI repression (since it’s detected in pachytene despite X silencing). This escape would allow it to act on the sex chromatin itself or elsewhere in the nucleus.
Plausible Drive Mechanism: SCML1’s dispensability for fertility yet rapid evolution suggests it might be engaged in a subtle intragenomic conflict. One possible mechanism: influencing sex chromosome inactivation/reactivation dynamics to favor X-bearing gametes. SCML1 could help maintain the silenced state of the X and Y during pachytene (ensuring meiotic progression), but perhaps it more effectively represses Y-linked genes than X-linked ones. If SCML1 preferentially localizes to the Y (for instance, binding methylated H3K9 on Y chromatin) and keeps Y genes off, any leaky expression from the Y (which can be toxic at pachytene) would be curtailed in X-bearing spermatocytes as well (since those cells have SCML1 to act on their paired Y segment). However, Y-bearing spermatocytes – which also have the single X with Scml1 gene – do produce SCML1, so that exact mechanism doesn’t differentiate between X or Y during meiosis; both types have SCML1 present. Another angle: SCML1 might regulate genomic imprinting or gene expression in the gamete that affects early embryo development in a parent-of-origin manner. For example, SCML1 might deposit repressive marks on certain genes in sperm. If SCML1-marked loci in X-bearing sperm vs Y-bearing differ (perhaps SCML1 targets an autosomal gene that interacts with sex of the embryo), the viability of embryos could skew. This is highly conjectural. More straightforward: SCML1’s adaptive evolution hints it might be a target of suppression or competition – maybe a driver element that was counteracted by suppressors. Scml1 knockout being fertile might indicate that whatever drive it caused has been suppressed genetically. Thus, SCML1 could historically have caused minor segregation distortion which is now neutralized, making knockout innocuous. For instance, SCML1 protein on the Y chromosome during male meiosis might inadvertently cause a slight arrest or delay specifically when it binds Y (e.g., by over-repressing a needed Y gene). If a suppressor on Y evolved to mitigate that, SCML1’s drive effect is hidden. But in species where suppressor isn’t present, SCML1 drive might manifest. Summarily, SCML1 is a candidate for historical meiotic drive – currently “limbo” in effect due to evolutionary suppression, but signaled by its rapid evolution in primates.
Supporting Literature: Hamer et al. (2008) discovered SCML1 in rat dense bodies and created Scml1-null mice, finding no fertility effect . They labeled the dense body and SCML1 function as “enigmatic” but not required. However, a 2019 BMC Evolutionary Biology paper by Godfrey et al. showed that SCML1 exhibits patterns of positive selection in primates, consistent with roles in sperm competition or genomic conflict . They hypothesized SCML1 is involved in spermatogenesis during sexual maturation. Additionally, the fact that SCML1 is part of polycomb pathways implies it might redundantly ensure gene silencing during meiosis (parallel to other polycomb like SCML2, which is required for proper sex chromosome repression). SCML2 (autosomal) actually is crucial: knockout of Scml2 in mice causes mis-expression of sex-linked genes and meiotic arrest. One might speculate SCML1 and SCML2 have overlapping targets, with SCML2 being the essential one and SCML1 providing an extra layer on the X – perhaps tipping the balance in subtle ways that could have led to drive. If SCML1’s effect was too strong, it might have been counter-selected or suppressed, which aligns with its now non-essential status. Therefore, SCML1 is a fascinating example of an X-linked gene with all the hallmarks of a driver (testis-specific, rapidly evolving, chromatin-modifying) yet whose current loss causes no obvious defect due to likely suppression – a “cryptic driver” scenario.

title: SRPX2 (Sushi-Repeat-Containing Protein, X-linked 2)
content:
Function: SRPX2 is a secreted protein containing three Sushi (CCP) repeats, which are protein interaction motifs commonly found in complement and adhesion proteins. SRPX2 has been implicated in neuronal development – it promotes synapse formation, particularly in speech-related areas of the cortex . Mutations in SRPX2 cause Rolandic epilepsy and speech apraxia. In cancer, SRPX2 can function in angiogenesis and cell migration; for instance, it’s upregulated in certain carcinomas and enhances NF-κB signaling in thyroid cancer cells . As a secreted ECM-associated protein, SRPX2 can bind receptors or matrix components to influence cell behavior (e.g., it binds uPAR, the urokinase receptor, affecting cell migration).
